title: Cat Extreme Sensitivity to Touch Along Spine: Causes & Triage
meta_desc: Learn why your cat is sensitive along their spine, if it is an emergency, and how photos/videos help your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
What is Spinal Sensitivity in Cats?

Extreme sensitivity along a cat's spine, often called 'rippling skin syndrome,' is frequently associated with Feline Hyperesthesia Syndrome (FHS). This condition causes the skin along the back to twitch or ripple, often leading to sudden bursts of grooming, running, or even mild aggression when the area is touched. Other causes can include osteoarthritis, spinal injuries, or dermatological issues like flea allergy dermatitis.

Is This an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. Generally, if your cat is still eating, drinking, and moving all four limbs normally, this is not a life-threatening emergency. While the behavior can be startling, it is usually a chronic condition that can be managed during a scheduled veterinary visit. However, if your cat suddenly loses the use of their back legs or seems in uncontrollable pain, seek immediate care.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a video of the sensitivity episode or a high-quality photo of the skin along the spine is incredibly helpful for triage. Cats often hide their symptoms at the vet due to stress. A visual record allows the veterinarian to see the exact nature of the twitching or rippling and check for signs of skin irritation or hair loss that might point toward a specific diagnosis like parasites or allergies.

faqs.question: What are the signs of Feline Hyperesthesia?
faqs.answer: Common signs include skin rippling, sudden intense grooming of the back or tail, dilated pupils, and frantic running.

faqs.question: Can arthritis cause spine sensitivity?
faqs.answer: Yes, inflammation in the spinal joints (osteoarthritis) can make the surrounding muscles very tense and painful to the touch.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat at home?
faqs.answer: Avoid touching the sensitive area to prevent stress, and ensure they have a quiet, low-stress environment until you can see a vet.
